coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and characterization of graphene quantum dots thin film for reducing cross-sectional heat transfer through smart window
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Tam D.
Yeo, Loo Pin
Si Yang, Kiw
Chiew Kei, Tan
Wang, Zhiwei
Mandler, Daniel
Magdassi, Shlomo
Tok, Alfred Iing Yoong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene and its derivatives have been reported as materials with excellent electrical and thermal conductivity, allowing for various promising applications. In particular, the large-scale surface coating of graphene-based materials can be employed to minimize cross-sectional heat transfer through the glass window. This study introduces a facile and cost-effective method to fabricate graphene quantum dots (GQDs) thin film on Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) glass via casting of the GQDs dispersion and stabilizing with poly-vinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP). The thin film possesses excellent optical properties of GQDs and allows more than 80 % of visible transmittance. The presence of the GQDs thin film shows effective reduction in the cross-sectional thermal diffusivity of FTO glass, from 0.55 mm2/s to zero when measured with laser flash over a 4-second period. This low cost and eco-friendly GQDs thin film will be a promising material for heat management in smart window applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Poly-Vinyl-Pyrrolidone
Visible transmittance
Cross-Sectional heat transfer
Smart window

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface morphology and chemistry of GQDs thin films
Optical properties of GQDs thin films and its effect on thermal diffusivity through FTO glass

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image) and (b) size distribution of GQDs.
(a) XRD patterns) and (b) FTIR spectra of PVP and GQDs thin films.
AFM images of (a) FTO, (b) PVP, (c) GQDs thin film and (d) corresponding surface roughness.
Wide scan XPS spectra (a) of PVP and GQDs thin films. The fine XPS spectra of O 1s (b), C 1s (c) and N 1s (d) of GQDs thin films.
Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs thin films with 360 nm excitation beam (a) and 300–400 nm excitation beams (c and d) with various GQDs concentrations.
(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) visible light transmittance of PVP and GQDs thin films.
Thermal diffusivity through FTO, FTO/PVP and FTO/GQDs thin films with 10 (a and b) and 50 mg (c and d) of PVP at 25 (a–c) and 40 °C (b and d).
Synthetic procedure of GQDs thin film.
Surface elemental composition of PVP and GQDs thin film by XPS quantification analysis.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and characterization of graphene quantum dots thin film for reducing cross-sectional heat transfer through smart window

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene and its derivatives have been reported as materials with excellent electrical and thermal conductivity, allowing for various promising applications. In particular, the large-scale surface coating of graphene-based materials can be employed to minimize cross-sectional heat transfer through the glass window. This study introduces a facile and cost-effective method to fabricate graphene quantum dots (GQDs) thin film on Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) glass via casting of the GQDs dispersion and stabilizing with poly-vinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP). The thin film possesses excellent optical properties of GQDs and allows more than 80 % of visible transmittance. The presence of the GQDs thin film shows effective reduction in the cross-sectional thermal diffusivity of FTO glass, from 0.55 mm2/s to zero when measured with laser flash over a 4-second period. This low cost and eco-friendly GQDs thin film will be a promising material for heat management in smart window applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based materials, with their excellent electrical and thermal conductivities, are promising for a variety of applications in wearable electronic devices and heat management systems, among others [1–15]. These materials can also be employed in smart window technology for thermal management in buildings, which aims to lower the energy consumption of air-conditioning. Heat generally penetrates through the glass windows via the effect of thermal radiation and heat conduction. While the transmittance of thermal radiation (mostly from the infrared range) can be modulated, most commonly by electrochromic materials, the modulation of heat conduction through the glass window has not been as widely studied. The heat transfer between any two systems is driven by a temperature gradient. A higher external temperature will cause an increased heat transfer into the room, resulting in higher energy consumption of air-conditioning. For the buildings assembled with many large-scale glass window panels, the heat conduction through window can become significantly despite the thermal conductivity of glass (with a typical thickness of 3 mm) is relatively low, about 0.9 W/mK [16]. Generally, the heat conduction could be easily blocked by using a double or triple glazed window, with an insulating air gap between two panes [17,18]. However, such windows are often bulky and costly to fabricate. Therefore, we propose a new strategy for heat management through glass windows by coating a thin layer of graphene- or graphene oxide-based material [19]. This thin film, with its high lateral thermal conductivity, can minimize the cross-sectional thermal diffusivity through glass windows and effectively delay the heat transfer. The heat that was conducted to the edge of the window pane can be removed by a cooling system or heat sink (Scheme S1).

In large-scale smart window applications, graphene-based materials have various drawbacks in terms of their fabrication costs, low visible transparency and issues with homogenous, large-area surface grafting. These drawbacks can be solved by using graphene-derived materials, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or graphene nanoplatelets, which also have excellent thermal conductivity [20–25], catalytic activity [26,27] as well as optical properties [28–30]. In the case of GQDs, the advantages include non-toxicity, simple surface grafting and the possibility of large-scale industrial production. Nonetheless, previous procedures of GQDs synthesis involved high-cost raw materials such as graphene [31,32] or photonic crystals [34] and made use of fairly low-yield and expensive methods such as laser ablation [35], electron beam lithography [36], electrochemical synthesis [37], or photo-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide (GO) [38,39]. Some studies have successfully synthesized GQDs from coal or graphite flake (the least expensive material known), increasing the possibility of using GQDs in future commercial products [40–43]. Alternatively, GQDs can also be prepared from fairly inexpensive and abundant organic sources such as citric acid or urea for product cost reduction [24,25,44–46]. Due to their low production costs, GQDs derived from organic sources are not only feasible for large-scale industrial applications but can potentially be used as a cost-effective and eco-friendly alternative to conventional graphene. In the field of heat management in windows, the coating and stabilizing of GQDs on glass window surfaces have not yet been widely reported.

In this study, we examine the fabrication of GQDs thin film via a low cost, facile drop-casting method on FTO glass and further characterized its morphological and optical properties. Additionally, its effect on reducing cross-sectional thermal diffusivity through FTO glass was also measured by laser flash.

The GQDs were obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid in the air, followed by a casting process on FTO glass in the presence of poly-vinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP) as a binding agent and surfactant (surface stabilization component). The GQDs thin film was successfully fabricated and possesses excellent optical properties similar to those of original GQDs allowing more than 80 % of visible transmittance. The cross-sectional thermal diffusivity through FTO glass was also reduced once coated with the GQDs film. A laser flash measurement was carried out over a period of 4 s and the cross-sectional thermal diffusivity of FTO of ca. 0.55 mm2/s was reduced to zero in the presence of the GQDs thin film. The organically-derived GQDs thin film is not only low cost and eco-friendly, its excellent optical properties and reduced cross-sectional thermal diffusivity have also proven it to be promising for heat management in smart window applications.

The synthesis of GQDs thin films is presented in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the GQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich) in a flask heated in a silicone oil bath. The citric acid is heated at 200 °C in air for 0.5 h until a dark orange solution is formed. 10 mg of PVP (Sigma-Aldrich, MW ∼ 1,300,000) was dissolved in 100 mL of DI water in a separate beaker and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, the as-synthesized GQDs were transferred into the PVP solution using a glass pipette. The GQDs-PVP solution was stirred and ultra-sonicated for 30 min. each to obtain a homogenous dispersion. Finally, the GQDs thin films were obtained by drop-casting the precursor solution onto fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass and dried at 60 °C in air for 72 h. For comparison, different concentrations of GQDs (1, 2, and 3 g) were used to fabricate the thin film.

A JEOL 2010 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was employed for HRTEM image analysis of the synthesized GQDs. The GQDs samples were dispersed in ethanol solution (1:100 vol ratio) and sonicated before being dropped onto the amorphous carbon-coated copper grid. The dot size was evaluated from the HRTEM images using the ImageJ software.

The crystal structure and surface morphology of the GQDs thin film were also characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu XRD-6000, with the λ Cu-Kα =0.15418 nm) and an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Asylum Research Cypher S) via the AC Air Topography mode.

A Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Spectrum) was used to measure the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of as-fabricated GQDs thin films. Their surface chemistry was characterized by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Kratos AXIS Supra). The optical properties of the GQDs thin films were characterized by the photoluminescence (Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer) and the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra (Cary 500 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer).

The thermal diffusivity of FTO/GQDs thin film samples was measured with a DLF 1200 laser flash equipment (TA instrument) in Ar atmosphere. A graphite layer was sprayed on both sides of the glass sample before measurement. In principle, the laser flash method measures the increase in temperature over time on one side of a plane-parallel sample, with a laser pulse generating heat from the other side. Although the thin film was only coated on the conductive side of the FTO glass, the laser flash measurements were taken on both sides of the glass to determine the effect of the film. The “up” measurement is recorded when the laser source irradiates onto the non-conductive side and the “down” measurement is recorded when the laser source irradiates onto the thin film side (Schem. S2). The efficacy of QGDs thin film in reducing cross-sectional thermal diffusivity through glass substrates can be determined through a comparison of these measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and chemistry of GQDs thin films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the HRTEM image and dots size distribution of as-synthesized GQDs, which were successfully formed after the pyrolysis process of citric acid at 200 °C. The high crystallinity of GQDs can be determined by the presence of its fine lattice structure, with a lattice parameter of approximately 0.21 nm. The dot size is small and uniform, with an average diameter of ca. 4.8 (±0.8) nm. The small size of the GQDs improves the homogeneity of the thin-film precursor solution during its dispersion in DI-water/PVP solution, thereby increasing the uniformity of the final thin film. However, the size also has an influence on the distribution of GQDs, which will subsequently determine the lateral thermal conductivity of the thin films as the proposed mechanism is because of the proximity / connectivity / overlap of the GQDs. Generally, by varying the concentration of GQDs, the drop-casted thin films show a difference in apparent color (Fig. S1). The GQDs thin films are light yellow, which becomes darker with increasing GQDs concentration. Additionally, the thickness, as measured with a surface profiler, is measured to be approximately 1.5, 8.1 and 11.2 μm for the GQDs thin film with 1, 2, 3 g concentration of GQDs, respectively (Fig. S2). Although a lower concentration of GQDs in the film can increase its transparency, the GQDs connectivity would be disrupted (i.e. the large distance between GQDs), thus reducing the effectiveness of the film in reducing the cross-sectional thermal diffusivity. Therefore, the distribution and concentration of GQDs in DI-water/PVP solution have to be optimized.

Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of FTO, PVP, and GQDs thin films. The FTO substrate exhibits characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26.7°, 33.8°, 38.0°, 51.7°, 55.0°, 61.7°, 66.1°, 78.7°, corresponding to the planes of (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (310), (301) and (321) of SnO2 (ICDD 01-070-4176), respectively. As observed from the pattern, the coating of PVP does not affect the crystal structure, in particular, the diffraction peak intensity of FTO glass. However, the intensity of the SnO2 diffraction peaks is significantly reduced when coated with GQDs thin films. This could be due to obstruction by the layers of GQDs. The diffraction peak of (002) plane of GQDs overlaps with the peak of (110) plane of SnO2, but can still be observed at 2θ = 26.5°. Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectra of PVP and GQDs thin films. The main IR vibrational assignments in the PVP thin film, as supported by previous reports, are denoted in Fig. 2b. It comprises of OH stretching vibration at 3450 cm−1, C–H asymmetric stretching vibration at 2955 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1661 cm−1 and CN vibration at 1018 cm−1, respectively [47,48]. For the GQDs thin films, the FTIR spectra show the absorption of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, as evidenced by the COO− symmetric stretching vibration at 1388 cm−1 and the COO− antisymmetric stretching vibration at 1571 cm−1 [45,49,50]. This implies that the GQDs are successfully dispersed within the PVP film.

Fig. 3 presents the atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of bare FTO, PVP and GQDs thin films. The surfaces of FTO and PVP thin films are observed to be much more uniform than that of the GQDs thin films. The average surface roughness of all samples is shown in Fig. 3d. The FTO substrate and PVP thin films have a smooth surface, with a surface roughness of approximately 11.9 and 22.2 nm respectively, while the GQDs thin film has a large surface roughness of approximately 217.6 nm. This may be due to the unstable solvent evaporation rate of the drop-casting method or the mismatch between GQDs and PVP in as-fabricated thin films. The high surface roughness can negatively affect the thermal conductivity of GQDs thin films and therefore, it has to be minimized [51].

Fig. 4 shows the XPS spectra of GQDs thin film. The wide scan spectra (Fig. 4a) demonstrate the presence of C1 s, O1 s and N1 s peaks in both PVP and GQDs thin films. It is observed that the peak intensity of N1 s component of GQDs thin films is comparatively lower while the peak intensity of O1 s is higher as compared to that of the PVP thin films. The detailed elemental composition is shown in Table 1 . From the quantification analysis, the O/C atomic ratio in GQDs thin films (1:2) is found to be much higher than that in PVP thin films (∼1:8). This may be due to the presence of more COOH and −COH functional groups of GQDs in the thin films. Fine XPS measurements are used to further analyze these elements in GQDs thin films, as shown in Fig. 4c-d. By curve-fitting analysis (Gauss*Lorentz Algorithm), the C1 s spectrum indicates that GQDs thin films have 3 main types of C atoms: CC/C = C (∼284.8 eV), COC (∼286.4 eV), and CO/COOH (289.6 eV). The intense peak at 284.8 eV can be attributed to carbon with sp2 and sp3 hybridization accompanied by two shoulders at higher binding energy which originated from oxygen linkages [15,33,43]. The best fitting for O1 s and N1 s indicates that GQDs thin films have two types of O atoms (organic CO (∼532.6 eV) and CO (∼534 eV)), and one type of N atoms (CNH2 (∼400 eV)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of GQDs thin films and its effect on thermal diffusivity through FTO glass
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 presents the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of as-fabricated GQDs thin films with various GQDs concentrations. When excited by the UV beam at 360 nm, all three GQDs samples emitted blue light at 464 nm. This is relatively similar to the photoluminescence of GQDs solution prepared by citric pyrolysis and implies that the presence of PVP does not alter the optical properties of GQDs [45,46]. Interestingly, with 1 and 2 g concentration within the films, the emission wavelength of GQDs thin films is almost independent of the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 5b and c). Dong et al. also confirmed the near excitation-independence of the PL spectra of the GQDs obtained by citric acid carbonization. The uniformity of the size and surface state of sp2 clusters contained in GQDs is the reason for such excitation-independent emission [45]. These results may also indicate the relatively large distance between GQDs at low concentration in the thin films, resulting in such similar PL spectra of GQDs dispersed solution. On the contrary, the maximum emission wavelength of 3 g GQDs thin film is strongly dependent on the excitation wavelength, shifting from 464 to 480 nm when the excitation wavelength is increased gradually from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 5d). This trend observed in the PL spectra is similar to that observed in pure graphene or graphene oxide [52]. With higher density of GQDs in the thin film, there is better connectivity between the GQDs which forms a graphene-like structure. This feature is expected to enhance the lateral thermal conductivity of the as-fabricated GQDs thin films.

Fig. 6 shows the UV–vis absorbance and visible light transmittance spectra of the PVP and GQDs thin films. The PVP thin films do not exhibit any absorption peaks in the range of 300–900 nm. However, the GQDs thin films show an absorption peak at approximately 350 nm, which is in agreement with the UV–vis absorption behavior of GQDs solution [45,46]. The onset of the absorption peak, and thereof peak intensity, increases with higher GQDs concentrations in the GQDs thin film (Fig. 6a). The visible transparency of all samples is shown in Fig. 6b. The PVP thin films allow 100 % transmittance of the visible light within the range of 400–800 nm. The GQDs thin film also demonstrates excellent visible transparency, allowing at least 85 %, 80 % and 75 % visible light transmittance for the samples containing 1, 2, and 3 g of GQDs, respectively.

The effect of GQDs thin films on the cross-sectional heat transfer through FTO glass is measured with the laser flash technique and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . The GQDs thin films with 3 g concentration of GQDs were selected for investigation due to the higher connectivity of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 7a, the thermal diffusivities through FTO and FTO/PVP thin films are almost similar for both tested sides. The “up” and “down” thermal diffusivities are measured to be approximately 0.57 and 0.55 mm2/s for the FTO and, 0.53 and 0.54 mm2/s for the PVP thin film, respectively. With the coating of GQDs thin films, the thermal diffusivity through FTO is slightly reduced in the “up” measurement, i.e., ca. 0.51 mm2/s. As for the “down” measurement, there was no heat detected across the FTO glass coating with GQDs thin films within the measured timeframe (4 s). The presence of GQDs thin film reveals a significant reduction in cross-sectional heat conduction across the FTO substrate. It is hypothesized that as heat is absorbed by the GQDs, it is preferentially conducted laterally along the highly connected GQDs pathways as opposed to cross-sectional conduction through the FTO. At 40 °C, the same trend in thermal diffusivity of all samples is also observed. Even with a higher concentration of PVP (50 mg) in the composite thin film, no changes in the thermal diffusivity trend was observed (Fig. 7c and d). Current results confirm that GQDs contribute significantly to the observed heat transfer minimization effect. The facile and cost-effective casting of GQDs thin films on FTO glass has been demonstrated and can be employed for large scale surface coating of the glass windows to reduce the cross-sectional heat diffusivity. The heat conduction mechanism, as well as the lateral thermal diffusivity of as-fabricated GQD films, will subsequently be studied in detail using a customized, controlled heat conduction unit with an integrated heat sink.

In this study, a facile and cost-effective method to fabricate GQDs thin films on FTO glass has been developed. PVP, a transparent polymer, was found to be capable of stabilizing GQDs on the FTO glass surface. The as-synthesized GQDs thin films possess the excellent optical property of GQDs (with more than 80 % of visible transparency) and also demonstrate a reduction in cross-sectional thermal diffusivity through FTO glass substrate from 0.55 mm2/s to zero within a period of 4 s. The low cost and eco-friendly GQDs-based thin film is thus a promising material for the heat modulation in smart window applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by grants from the National Research Foundation, Prime Minister’s Office, Singapore under its Campus of Research Excellence and Technological Enterprise (CREATE) Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2020.110861.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanotube ballistic thermal conductance and its limits

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Mingo, D. A. Broido. Carbon Nanotube Ballistic Thermal Conductance and Its Limits. Phys. Rev. Lett. 2005, 95, 096105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Superior thermal conductivity of single-layer graphene

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal conductivity of isotopically modified graphene

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal properties of graphene: fundamentals and applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal properties of graphene and multilayer graphene: applications in thermal interface materials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal chemical vapor deposition grown graphene heat spreader for thermal management of hot spots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene thermal properties: applications in thermal management and energy storage

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal conductivity of graphene and graphite: collective excitations and mean free paths

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison on thermal transport properties of graphene and phosphorene nanoribbons

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Switch on the high thermal conductivity of graphene paper

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Measurement of specific heat and thermal conductivity of supported and suspended graphene by a comprehensive Raman optothermal method

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring the thermal and mechanical properties of graphene film by structural engineering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Wang, M. K. Samani, H. Li, L. Dong, Z. Zhang, P. Su, S. Chen, J. Chen, S. Huang, G. Yuan, X. Xu, B. Li, K. Leifer, L. Ye, J. Liu. Tailoring the Thermal and Mechanical Properties of Graphene Film by Structural Engineering. Small 2018, 14, 1801346.
M. Rubin. Calculating heat transfer through windows. Energy Res. 1982, 6, 341-349.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solar radiation glazing factors for window panes, glass structures and electrochromic windows in buildings – measurement and calculation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. P. Jelle. Solar radiation glazing factors for window panes, glass structures and electrochromic windows in buildings – Measurement and calculation. Solar Energy Materials & Solar Cells 2013, 116, 291–323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation of heat transfer in a triple-glazing type window at greek climate conditions
